lessons.title: 1.1 Why Python is a Top Placement Skill in 2026
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Understand Python's dominance in Backend, AI, and Scripting roles in 2026.
  • Identify key companies and job roles that primarily use Python.
  • Recognize the "2026 Python Stack" (FastAPI, Pydantic, PyTorch) vs legacy stacks.
  • Articulate why "Python is slow" is no longer a valid blocker for modern engineering.

2. Intuitive Theory: The "Universal" Language

In 2026, Python is not just a scripting language; it is the operating system of the AI era. While languages like C++ or Rust win on raw CPU cycles, Python wins on developer cycles and ecosystem integration.

The "Glue" Concept

Implementing a Neural Network in C from scratch takes months. In Python, it takes lines. Python handles the high-level logic, while libraries like PyTorch call optimized C++/CUDA code under the hood. You get the speed of C with the ease of English.

Placement Perspective: Recruiters don't just look for "Python". They look for:
1. Backend Proficiency: (FastAPI, AsyncIO) - Building scalable APIs.
2. Data/AI Literacy: (Pandas, Simple ML) - Handling data-driven features.
3. Problem Solving: Using Python's concise syntax to crush coding interviews (LeetCode/DSA).

3. Syntax & Code Patterns

Python 2026 relies on Type Hinting and Modern Syntax.

modern_vs_legacy.py
# Legacy Python (Pre-2020 style) - AVOID IN INTERVIEWS
def get_user(id):
    return {"id": id, "name": "Unknown"}

# Modern Python (2026 style) - DO THIS
from typing import TypedDict, Optional

class User(TypedDict):
    id: int
    name: str

def get_user(user_id: int) -> User:
    return {"id": user_id, "name": "Unknown"}

4. Real-Life Industry Use Cases

1. AI Wrappers

Startups allow users to "chat with PDF". The backend is Python (FastAPI) which receives the PDF, chunks it (LangChain), and sends it to OpenAI. This is 90% of new startups in 2026.

2. High-Frequency Trading

While the core execution engine is C++, the strategy logic, data analysis, and backtesting are all Python (NumPy/Pandas) due to development speed.

5. Variants & Advanced Notes

CPython vs. PyPy vs. Mojo:
Most jobs use standard CPython.
PyPy is a JIT compiler alternative for pure Python speed.
Mojo is a new superset of Python for AI hardware, gaining traction in 2026 for performance-critical AI code. Stick to CPython for placements unless asked.

lessons.title: 1.2 Setting Up Python 3.11+ (Environment Mastery)
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Install Python 3.11+ correctly on Windows/Mac/Linux.
  • Understand the difference between System Python and User Python.
  • Master venv for isolated project environments (Critical for avoiding "Dependency Hell").
  • Configure VS Code with the Black formatter and Pylance.

2. Intuitive Theory: The "Environment" Problem

Imagine Project A needs Django 2.0 and Project B needs Django 4.0. If you install libraries globally, they overwrite each other.
Solution: Virtual Environments. Think of a Virtual Environment (venv) as a "sandbox". Every project gets its own playground with its own toys (libraries).

3. Syntax & Code Patterns

Creating a Venv (Windows)

setup_windows.ps1
# 1. Check version
python --version

# 2. Create venv (named 'venv')
python -m venv venv

# 3. Activate
.\venv\Scripts\activate

# 4. Verify (Where is python running from?)
where python
# Should point to ...\your_project\venv\Scripts\python.exe

Creating a Venv (Mac/Linux)

setup_unix.sh
# 1. Create
python3 -m venv venv

# 2. Activate
source venv/bin/activate

4. Common Mistakes

  • Mistake: Installing everything with `pip install x` globally.
    Fix: Always activate venv first.
  • Mistake: Committing the `venv` folder to GitHub.
    Fix: Add `venv/` to your `.gitignore` file.

lessons.title: 1.3 First Steps: Scripts vs REPL
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Execute code via Interactive Shell (REPL) vs Script Files (.py).
  • Master Input/Output with `print()` and `input()`.
  • Write effective single-line and docstring comments.

2. Intuitive Theory: The REPL

REPL stands for Read, Eval, Print, Loop. It's Python's "Instant Messenger". You type a line, Python replies immediately.
Great for testing small logic (e.g., "What does this slice do?").
Scripts (.py) are for permanent programs.

3. Syntax & Code Patterns

io_basics.py
# --- Output ---
print("Hello", "World", sep=" | ", end="!!\n")
# Output: Hello | World!!

# --- Input ---
# input() always returns a STRING
age_str = input("Enter age: ") 
# You must cast it to do math
age = int(age_str)

# --- Comments ---
# Single line comment

"""
Multi-line Docstring.
Usually used to describe a module or function.
"""

4. Worked Example: The Greeting Bot

bot.py
def greeting_bot():
    print("--- WELCOME SYSTEM ---")
    
    # getting inputs
    name = input("Username: ")
    # Chained conversion
    year = int(input("Year of Birth: ")) 
    
    # Logic
    current_year = 2026
    age = current_year - year
    
    # f-string formatting (The modern way)
    print(f"Access Granted. User: {name.upper()} | Age: {age}")

# Executing
greeting_bot()

Note: We used f"Content {variable}". This is called an f-string. Never use "Age: " + str(age) in 2026. It's slow and unreadable.

lessons.title: 1.4 Variables & Data Types
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Understand Dynamic Typing in Python (Variables are tags, not boxes).
  • Master the Primitives: `int`, `float`, `str`, `bool`.
  • Understand Immutability fundamentals.

2. Intuitive Theory: Variables as Tags

In C++, `int a = 10` creates a box named `a` and puts `10` inside. In Python, `10` is an object in memory. `a` is just a sticky note (reference) attached to it.
If you do `a = 20`, you aren't changing the `10`. You are moving the sticky note to a new object `20`.

3. Syntax & Code Patterns

types.py
# --- Primitives ---
x = 10          # int (arbitrary precision in Python 3!)
y = 3.14        # float (double precision C-double)
name = 'Flyeng' # str
is_active = True # bool

# --- Type Checking ---
print(type(x)) # 

# --- Type Conversion ---
price = "99.99"
total = float(price) * 2 
print(int(total)) # 199 (Truncates decimal)

4. Edge Case: Float Precision

Floats are binary approximations.

float_error.py
print(0.1 + 0.2)
# Output: 0.30000000000000004
# Use 'decimal' module for money!

lessons.title: 1.5 Operators & Expressions
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Distinguish between Division `/` and Floor Division `//`.
  • Use Logical Operators `and`, `or`, `not` (Short-circuiting).
  • Master Membership operators `in`, `not in`.
  • Understand Identity `is` vs Equality `==`.

2. Syntax & Code Patterns

Arithmetic

math_ops.py
a = 15
b = 4

print(a / b)   # 3.75 (Standard Division)
print(a // b)  # 3    (Floor/Integer Division)
print(a % b)   # 3    (Modulus/Remainder)
print(a ** b)  # 50625 (Power: 15^4)

Logical & Comparison

logic.py
# Chained Comparison (Python Exclusive Feature!)
x = 10
if 5 < x < 15:  # Same as: 5 < x and x < 15
    print("x is in range")

# Short Circuit
def expensive_check():
    print("Checking...")
    return True

# If first part is False, expensive_check() is NEVER run
if False and expensive_check():
    print("Won't happen")

3. Critical Interview Topic: `is` vs `==`

== checks Value (content).
is checks Identity (memory address).

identity.py
list_a = [1, 2, 3]
list_b = [1, 2, 3]

print(list_a == list_b) # True (Content is same)
print(list_a is list_b) # False (Two different objects in memory)

a = 10
b = 10
print(a is b) # True (Python internals cache small integers!)

lessons.title: 1.6 Control Flow 1: Conditionals
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Apply `if`, `elif`, `else` structures.
  • Understand "Truthiness" (Falsy values).
  • Use the Ternary Operator equivalent.

2. Intuitive Theory: Truthiness

In Python, empty structures evaluate to False. You don't need to check len(x) > 0.

  • Falsy: 0, 0.0, "", [], {}, None, False
  • Truthy: Everything else.

3. Syntax & Code Patterns

conditionals.py
users = []

if not users:  # Pythonic check for empty list
    print("No users found")
else:
    print(f"{len(users)} users active")

# Assignment Expressions (Walrus Operator :=) - Python 3.8+
# Calculates length, assigns it to n, and checks n > 3 in one line
if (n := len(users)) > 3:
    print(f"Too many users: {n}")

Ternary Conditional

ternary.py
status = "Adult" if age >= 18 else "Minor"

lessons.title: 1.7 Control Flow 2: Loops
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Master `for` loops with `range()` and iterables.
  • Use `while` loops for state-based iteration.
  • Control loops with `break`, `continue`, and the unique `else` block.

2. Syntax & Code Patterns

For Loops (The Iterator Pattern)

loops.py
# Range is [start, stop)
for i in range(1, 6, 2): 
    print(i) # 1, 3, 5

# Looping over collection
names = ["Alice", "Bob"]
for name in names:
    print(name)

# Using Index AND Value (enumerate) - INTERVIEW PATTERN
for idx, name in enumerate(names):
    print(f"Rank {idx+1}: {name}")

3. Advanced Feature: Loop `else`

The `else` block executes ONLY if the loop completed normally (i.e., NO `break` hit).

loop_else.py
# Search Pattern
target = 5
numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4]

for n in numbers:
    if n == target:
        print("Found!")
        break
else:
    print("Not found in list") 
# Helpful to avoid a 'found = False' flag variable

lessons.title: 1.8 Functions
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Define functions with `def`.
  • Understand Positional vs Keyword Arguments.
  • Master Default Arguments (and the Mutable Default Argument Trap).
  • Scope: LEGB Rule (Local, Enclosing, Global, Built-in).

2. Syntax & Code Patterns

functions.py
def calculate_total(price, tax_rate=0.05, discount=0):
    total = price * (1 + tax_rate) - discount
    return total

# Keyword usage (More readable)
val = calculate_total(price=100, discount=10)

# Unpacking Arguments (*args, **kwargs)
def super_sum(*args):
    return sum(args)

print(super_sum(10, 20, 30)) # 60

3. The Mutable Default Trap (Interview Favorite)

Never use a mutable object (list, dict) as a default argument.

WRONG
bad.py
def append_x(items=[]):
    items.append('x')
    return items

print(append_x()) # ['x']
print(append_x()) # ['x', 'x'] !!
# The list is created ONCE at definition time.
RIGHT
good.py
def append_x(items=None):
    if items is None:
        items = [] # New list every time
    items.append('x')
    return items

lessons.title: 1.9 Coding Style & PEP 8
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Write pythonic code adhering to PEP 8.
  • Naming conventions: `snake_case` vs `CamelCase`.
  • Import ordering.

2. Essential PEP 8 Rules

Element Convention Example
Functions / Variables snake_case calculate_tax
Classes CamelCase (PascalCase) UserProfile
Constants UPPER_SNAKE_CASE MAX_RETRIES
Protected/Private _leading_underscore _internal_helper

3. Import Ordering

Always group imports in this order:

imports.py
# 1. Standard Library
import os
import sys

# 2. Third Party
import pandas as pd
import requests

# 3. Local Application
from my_app.models import User
from my_app.utils import helper

lessons.title: 1.10 Mini Project: CLI Task Manager
lessons.content:
The Goal

Build a simple command-line Text Utility that reinforces: loops, conditionals, lists, and functions.

Complete Solution

task_manager.py
import sys

tasks = []

def show_menu():
    print("\n--- TASK MASTER 1.0 ---")
    print("1. Add Task")
    print("2. View Tasks")
    print("3. Remove Task")
    print("4. Exit")
    return input("Select Option (1-4): ")

def add_task():
    task = input("Enter Task Description: ").strip()
    if task:
        tasks.append(task)
        print("✅ Task Added!")
    else:
        print("⚠️ Cannot add empty task.")

def view_tasks():
    if not tasks:
        print("📭 No tasks yet.")
        return
    print("\n--- YOUR TASKS ---")
    for idx, t in enumerate(tasks, 1):
        print(f"{idx}. {t}")

def remove_task():
    view_tasks()
    if not tasks:
        return
        
    choice = input("Enter number to remove: ")
    if choice.isdigit():
        idx = int(choice) - 1
        if 0 <= idx < len(tasks):
            removed = tasks.pop(idx)
            print(f"🗑️ Removed: {removed}")
        else:
            print("❌ Invalid number.")
    else:
        print("❌ Please enter a number.")

def main():
    while True:
        choice = show_menu()
        
        if choice == '1':
            add_task()
        elif choice == '2':
            view_tasks()
        elif choice == '3':
            remove_task()
        elif choice == '4':
            print("Goodbye! 👋")
            break
        else:
            print("Invalid Option. Try again.")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

Why this matters?

This simple script uses:

  • while True for the main app loop.
  • enumerate for user-friendly listing.
  • input handling and validation (isdigit).
  • List methods (append, pop).
  • if __name__ == "__main__": block (Best practice for entry points).

lessons.title: 2.1 Strings: The Interview Bread & Butter
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Master String Immutability and why it matters for memory.
  • Perform complex Slicing `[start:stop:step]`.
  • Utilize essential methods: `split`, `join`, `strip`, `replace`.

2. Intuitive Theory: Strings are Tuples of Characters

In Python, strings are immutable. You cannot change a character in place.
s[0] = 'a' throws a TypeError.
To "change" a string, you create a new string. This is crucial for understanding time complexity (concatenation loops are O(N^2) if done poorly).

3. Syntax & Code Patterns

string_ops.py
text = "  Python Programming  "

# 1. Cleaning
clean = text.strip().lower() # "python programming"

# 2. Slicing [start : stop : step]
print(clean[0:6])   # "python"
print(clean[::-1])  # "gnimmargorp nohtyp" (Reverse - Interview Trick!)

# 3. Splitting & Joining (The most common op)
words = clean.split() # ['python', 'programming']
csv_row = ",".join(words) # "python,programming"

4. Common Mistake: String Concatenation

Slow (O(N^2))
slow.py
res = ""
for w in words:
    res += w + " "
Fast (O(N))
fast.py
# Use list join
res = " ".join(words)

lessons.title: 2.2 Lists: Dynamic Arrays
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Understand List internals (Dynamic Array mechanics).
  • Master List Comprehensions (The Pythonic signature).
  • Know the cost of operations: `pop(0)` vs `pop()`.

2. Intuitive Theory: Dynamic Arrays

Python lists are NOT linked lists. They are C arrays (contiguous memory) that resize automatically.
Append: O(1) amortized (fast).
Insert/Delete at start: O(N) (slow - everything shifts).

3. Syntax & Code Patterns

List Comprehensions

comprehension.py
nums = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]

# Goal: Square even numbers
# Old Way
res = []
for n in nums:
    if n % 2 == 0:
        res.append(n*n)

# Pythonic Way
# [expression for item in list if conditional]
squares = [n*n for n in nums if n % 2 == 0] 
print(squares) # [4, 16]

4. Visualizing Methods

list_perf.py
stack = [10, 20]
stack.append(30) # [10, 20, 30] - O(1)
top = stack.pop() # 30 - O(1)

queue_attempt = [10, 20]
# BAD PERFORMANCE for Queues
front = queue_attempt.pop(0) # 10 - O(N) Shift!
# Use collections.deque for Queues

lessons.title: 2.3 Tuples, Sets, and Frozensets
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Tuples: Immutable lists (Keys for dicts, Return values).
  • Sets: O(1) lookups and Mathematical operations.
  • Frozensets: Use a set as a key in a dict.

2. Comparison Table

TypeMutable?Ordered?Duplicates?Use Case
ListYesYesYesGeneral purpose
TupleNoYesYesFixed records, dict keys
SetYesNoNoUnique items, O(1) check

3. Syntax & Code Patterns

sets.py
# --- Set Logic (Venn Diagrams) ---
devs = {"Alice", "Bob"}
managers = {"Bob", "Charlie"}

print(devs.intersection(managers)) # {'Bob'}
print(devs.difference(managers))   # {'Alice'}
print(devs.union(managers))        # {'Alice', 'Bob', 'Charlie'}

# --- Deduplication Trick ---
ids = [1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 3]
unique_ids = list(set(ids)) # [1, 2, 3]

lessons.title: 2.4 Dictionaries: Hash Maps
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Understand Hashing and Key constraints (must be immutable/hashable).
  • Master `get()`, `setdefault()`, and iteration patterns.
  • Use Dictionary Comprehensions.

2. Syntax & Code Patterns

dicts.py
user = {"id": 1, "name": "Alice"}

# 1. Safe Access
# print(user["age"]) -> KeyError
print(user.get("age", 18)) # Returns 18 if 'age' missing

# 2. Iteration (Crucial for Interviews)
for k, v in user.items():
    print(f"{k} -> {v}")

# 3. Dict Comprehension
# Swap keys and values
codes = {"A": 1, "B": 2}
inverted = {v: k for k, v in codes.items()}
# {1: 'A', 2: 'B'}

4. Advanced: Defaultdict

Avoids the "check if key exists" initialization boilerplate.

grouping.py
from collections import default_dict

# Grouping words by first letter
words = ["apple", "apricot", "banana"]
grouped = default_dict(list)

for w in words:
    grouped[w[0]].append(w)
    
# {'a': ['apple', 'apricot'], 'b': ['banana']}

lessons.title: 2.5 Nested Structures
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Navigate 'JSON-like' structures (Lists of Dicts).
  • Handle Deep Copies vs Shallow Copies.

2. Worked Example: API Response Parse

nesting.py
data = {
    "users": [
        {"id": 1, "roles": ["admin", "editor"]},
        {"id": 2, "roles": ["viewer"]}
    ],
    "meta": {"page": 1}
}

# Goal: Get all roles of user 1
target_roles = []
for u in data["users"]:
    if u["id"] == 1:
        target_roles = u["roles"]
        break

print(target_roles) # ['admin', 'editor']

3. The Copy Trap

copy_trap.py
import copy

original = [[1, 2], [3, 4]]
shallow = original.copy() 

# Modifying inner list AFFECTS shallow copy
original[0][0] = 999 
print(shallow[0][0]) # 999 !!

# Use deepcopy for nested structures
deep = copy.deepcopy(original)

lessons.title: 2.6 Solving Classic Problems with Built-ins
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Replace 10 lines of loop code with 1 line of `collections.Counter`.
  • Solve "Two Sum" using a dictionary (O(N)).

2. The Power of Counter

counter.py
from collections import Counter

s = "leelee"
counts = Counter(s)
# Counter({'e': 4, 'l': 2})

# Top K Frequent elements
print(counts.most_common(1)) # [('e', 4)]

3. Two Sum (Interview Classic)

Problem: Find indices of two numbers adding to Target.

two_sum_opt.py
def two_sum(nums, target):
    seen = {} # map val -> index
    
    for i, num in enumerate(nums):
        diff = target - num
        if diff in seen:
            return [seen[diff], i]
        seen[num] = i
    return []

print(two_sum([2, 7, 11, 15], 9)) # [0, 1]

lessons.title: 2.7 Introduction to Time Complexity
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Understand Big O notation in the context of Python built-ins.
  • Memorize complexity of common operations.

2. Python Complexity Cheatsheet

OperationStructureComplexityNote
x in sListO(N)Linear Search
x in sSet/DictO(1)Hash Lookup
sort()ListO(N log N)Timsort
len(s)AnyO(1)Cached attribute
pop(0)ListO(N)Shift all elements

3. Why it matters?

optimization.py
# Finding common items

list1 = range(100000)
list2 = range(100000)

# BAD: O(N*M) - Quadratic
# if x in list2 -> scans list2 every time
common = [x for x in list1 if x in list2] 

# GOOD: O(N+M) - Linear
set2 = set(list2) # O(M)
# if x in set2 -> Instant O(1)
common = [x for x in list1 if x in set2]

lessons.title: 2.8 Mini Problem Set: Placement-Style
lessons.content:
Placement Challenge

Try solving these before looking at the code. These are Day 1 Placement Questions.

1. Valid Anagram

Given two strings s and t, return true if t is an anagram of s.

anagram.py
def is_anagram(s, t):
    # Pythonic One-Liner (O(N) space/time)
    return Counter(s) == Counter(t) 

    # Sorting approach (O(N log N) time, O(1) space)
    # return sorted(s) == sorted(t)

2. Group Anagrams

Group ["eat", "tea", "tan", "ate", "nat", "bat"]

group_anagrams.py
def group_anagrams(strs):
    groups = default_dict(list)
    
    for s in strs:
        # Key must be immutable (tuple)
        key = tuple(sorted(s)) 
        groups[key].append(s)
        
    return list(groups.values())

lessons.title: 3.1 File Handling: The 'With' Statement
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Read and Write text files safely.
  • Understand the Context Manager protocol (`with` statement).
  • Handle encoding issues (UTF-8).

2. Intuitive Theory: Resource Leaks

Opening a file asks the OS for a "file handle". If you don't close it, you leak resources.
The Old Way: Manually calling `f.close()`. Risk: If code crashes before close, file stays open.
The New Way: The `with` block auto-closes the file, ANY time you leave the block (even on error).

3. Syntax & Code Patterns

files.py
# Reading a file (Standard Pattern)
try:
    with open("data.txt", "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        content = f.read() # Reads ENTIRE file into RAM
        print(content)
except FileNotFoundError:
    print("File not found!")

# Reading line by line (Memory Safe for large files)
with open("large_log.txt", "r") as f:
    for line in f:
        process(line.strip())

# Writing (Overwrites existing!)
with open("output.txt", "w") as f:
    f.write("Hello File!\n")
    
# Appending
with open("log.txt", "a") as f:
    f.write("New Log Entry\n")

lessons.title: 3.2 Structured Data: JSON & CSV
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Parse and Dump JSON data (The language of APIs).
  • Read and Write CSV files representing tabular data.

2. JSON Handling

Serialization (Dump): Python Dict -> JSON String.
Deserialization (Load): JSON String -> Python Dict.

json_ops.py
import json

data = {
    "name": "Flyeng",
    "skills": ["Python", "AI"],
    "active": True
}

# 1. To String
json_str = json.dumps(data, indent=2) 
print(json_str)

# 2. To File
with open("config.json", "w") as f:
    json.dump(data, f, indent=4)

# 3. From String
parsed = json.loads('{"id": 1}')

3. CSV Handling

csv_ops.py
import csv

# Writing CSV
with open("students.csv", "w", newline="") as f:
    writer = csv.writer(f)
    writer.writerow(["Name", "Score"])
    writer.writerow(["Alice", 95])
    
# Reading as Dict (Best for Named Columns)
with open("students.csv", "r") as f:
    reader = csv.DictReader(f)
    for row in reader:
        print(f"{row['Name']} scored {row['Score']}")

lessons.title: 3.3 Exception Handling: Graceful Failure
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Handle crashes with `try`, `except`, `else`, `finally`.
  • Catch specific exceptions (Don't catch bare `Exception`).
  • Raise custom errors for business logic.

2. The Full Block

exceptions.py
def divide(a, b):
    try:
        result = a / b
    except ZeroDivisionError:
        print("Cannot divide by zero!")
        return None
    except TypeError as e:
        print(f"Wrong type: {e}")
        return None
    else:
        # Runs ONLY if no exception occurred
        print("Division successful")
        return result
    finally:
        # Runs ALWAYS (Cleanup)
        print("Op finished")

divide(10, 2)

3. Raising Errors

custom_error.py
def register(age):
    if age < 18:
        raise ValueError("Must be 18+")
    print("Welcome")
    
# Custom Exception
class InvalidTokenError(Exception):
    pass

lessons.title: 3.4 Modules & Packages
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Import from standard library, pip packages, and local files.
  • Understand `__init__.py` (Makes a folder a package).
  • Master `if __name__ == "__main__":`.

2. Project Structure

my_project/
├── main.py
├── requirements.txt
└── utils/
    ├── __init__.py
    └── math_tools.py

3. Code Pattern

imports.py
# In utils/math_tools.py
def add(a, b):
    return a + b

if __name__ == "__main__":
    # This runs ONLY when you run this file directly
    print("Testing add:", add(1, 2))

# In main.py
from utils.math_tools import add

print(add(10, 20)) 
# "Testing add..." will NOT print here.

lessons.title: 3.5 Pip & Virtual Environments
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Use `pip` to install/uninstall packages.
  • Freeze dependencies into `requirements.txt`.
  • Introduction to modern tools: `uv` (faster replacement).

2. Essential Commands

terminal.sh
# Install a package (e.g., requests)
pip install requests

# Save all current packages to file
pip freeze > requirements.txt

# Install from file (New Teammate Setup)
pip install -r requirements.txt

# --- Modern Era (uv) ---
# pip is slow. 'uv' is written in Rust and is 100x faster.
# uv pip install requests

lessons.title: 3.6 Debugging: Print vs Logging
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Stop using `print("here")` for production code.
  • Use the `logging` module for structured logs (Info, Warning, Error).
  • Basic VS Code Debugger usage (Breakpoints).

2. Why Logging?

Print goes to console and is lost. Logs can be saved to files, sent to dashboards (Datadog), and have severity levels.

logger.py
import logging

# Basic Setup
logging.basicConfig(
    level=logging.INFO,
    format='%(asctime)s - %(levelname)s - %(message)s'
)

def run_task():
    logging.info("Task started")
    try:
        x = 1 / 0
    except ZeroDivisionError:
        logging.error("Task failed: Division by zero", exc_info=True)

run_task()

lessons.title: 3.7 Mini Project: Validated Data Cleaner
lessons.content:
The Goal

Read a messy CSV of users, validate emails, and save clean rows to JSON. Logs errors to a file.

Complete Solution

cleaner.py
import csv
import json
import logging

# Setup Logging to File
logging.basicConfig(filename='cleaner.log', level=logging.INFO)

def is_valid_email(email):
    return "@" in email and "." in email

def clean_data(input_file, output_file):
    clean_rows = []
    
    try:
        with open(input_file, "r") as f:
            reader = csv.DictReader(f)
            
            for row in reader:
                email = row.get("email", "").strip()
                if is_valid_email(email):
                    clean_rows.append({
                        "name": row.get("name", "Unknown").title(),
                        "email": email
                    })
                else:
                    logging.warning(f"Skipping invalid email: {email}")

        with open(output_file, "w") as f:
            json.dump(clean_rows, f, indent=4)
            
        print(f"Successfully cleaned {len(clean_rows)} rows.")

    except FileNotFoundError:
        logging.critical("Input file missing!")
        print("Error: Input file not found.")

# Simulate usage
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Create dummy data
    with open("raw.csv", "w", newline="") as f:
        f.write("name,email\nalice,alice@gmail.com\nbob,bob_at_gmail")
    
    clean_data("raw.csv", "clean.json")

lessons.title: 4.1 Classes and Objects: The Blueprint
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Define Classes (Blueprints) and Objects (Instances).
  • Master the `__init__` constructor methods.
  • Understand `self` (The current instance).

2. Intuitive Theory: Blueprint vs House

Class: The architectural drawing of a house. (Requires no materials/memory).
Object: The actual house built from the drawing. (Takes up land/memory).
You can build 100 different houses from 1 blueprint.

3. Syntax & Code Patterns

basics_oop.py
class Car:
    # Constructor (Initializer)
    def __init__(self, model, color):
        self.model = model  # Attribute
        self.color = color
        self.speed = 0

    def drive(self):
        self.speed += 10
        print(f"{self.model} is going {self.speed} km/h")

# Creating Instances
tesla = Car("Model 3", "Red")
ford = Car("Mustang", "Blue")

tesla.drive() # Model 3 is going 10 km/h

lessons.title: 4.2 Methods: Instance vs Class vs Static
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Distinguish between Instance (`self`), Class (`cls`), and Static methods.
  • Use `@classmethod` for alternative constructors.
  • Use `@staticmethod` for utility functions inside a class.

2. Comparison Table

TypeDecoratorFirst ArgAccess
InstanceNoneselfThe Object
Class@classmethodclsThe Class itself
Static@staticmethodNoneNothing (Namespace only)

3. Syntax & Code Patterns

methods.py
class Date:
    def __init__(self, year, month, day):
        self.year = year
        self.month = month
        self.day = day

    # Alternative Constructor
    @classmethod
    def from_string(cls, date_str):
        # "2026-01-01" -> 2026, 1, 1
        year, month, day = map(int, date_str.split("-"))
        return cls(year, month, day)

    # Utility (No access to instance/class state)
    @staticmethod
    def is_valid_year(year):
        return 1900 <= year <= 3000

# Usage
d1 = Date(2026, 1, 1)
d2 = Date.from_string("2024-12-25")
print(Date.is_valid_year(2026)) # True

lessons.title: 4.3 Encapsulation & Properties
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Implement Private variables with `_` and `__`.
  • Use `@property` for Getters and Setters (Pythonic Encapsulation).
  • Avoid Java-style `get_x()` and `set_x()`.

2. Intuitive Theory: No True Privacy

Python assumes "We are all consenting adults".
_variable: Please don't touch (Convention).
__variable: Name Munging (Harder to touch, but possible).

3. Syntax & Code Patterns

encapsulation.py
class BankAccount:
    def __init__(self, balance):
        self._balance = balance # "Protected"

    @property
    def balance(self):
        # Getter
        print("Log: Checking balance")
        return self._balance

    @balance.setter
    def balance(self, value):
        # Setter w/ Validation
        if value < 0:
            raise ValueError("Balance cannot be negative")
        self._balance = value

acc = BankAccount(100)
acc.balance = 50   # Calls setter
print(acc.balance) # Calls getter -> 50
# acc.balance = -10 -> ValueError

lessons.title: 4.4 Inheritance & super()
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Create Child classes that Inherit from Parent classes.
  • Override methods to change behavior.
  • Use `super()` to reuse parent logic.

2. Syntax & Code Patterns

inheritance.py
class Animal:
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name

    def speak(self):
        pass # Abstract in spirit

class Dog(Animal):
    def speak(self):
        return "Woof!"

class Cat(Animal):
    def __init__(self, name, color):
        # Call Parent Constructor
        super().__init__(name) 
        self.color = color

    def speak(self):
        return "Meow!"

pets = [Dog("Buddy"), Cat("Luna", "Black")]
for p in pets:
    print(f"{p.name}: {p.speak()}")

lessons.title: 4.5 Polymorphism & Duck Typing
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Understand "If it walks like a duck...".
  • Write functions that accept interfaces, not specific types.

2. Intuitive Theory

In Java/C++, you must define interfaces. In Python, you just call the method. If the object has the method, it works. If not, it crashes (AttributeError).

3. Syntax & Code Patterns

polymorphism.py
class PDFParser:
    def parse(self):
        print("Parsing PDF...")

class HTMLParser:
    def parse(self):
        print("Parsing HTML...")

class Car:
    def drive(self): pass

def run_parser(parser):
    # Does not care if it is PDF or HTML
    # As long as it has .parse(), it works
    parser.parse()

run_parser(PDFParser())  # Works
run_parser(HTMLParser()) # Works
# run_parser(Car())      # AttributeError: 'Car' object has no attribute 'parse'

lessons.title: 4.6 Special (Dunder) Methods
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Customize object String representation (`__str__`, `__repr__`).
  • Enable Operator Overloading (`__add__`, `__eq__`, `__len__`).

2. Essential Dunders

MethodTriggerPurpose
__str__print(obj)User-friendly string
__repr__REPL / DebugDev-friendly string
__eq__a == bEquality check

3. Worked Example: Vector Class

vector.py
class Vector:
    def __init__(self, x, y):
        self.x = x
        self.y = y

    def __add__(self, other):
        # Enables v1 + v2
        return Vector(self.x + other.x, self.y + other.y)

    def __str__(self):
        return f"({self.x}, {self.y})"

    def __eq__(self, other):
        return self.x == other.x and self.y == other.y

v1 = Vector(2, 4)
v2 = Vector(1, 2)
v3 = v1 + v2

print(v3) # (3, 6)
print(v3 == Vector(3, 6)) # True

lessons.title: 4.7 Design Patterns: Strategy (Function-Based)
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Implement the Strategy Pattern using First-Class Functions (No extra classes needed).
  • Understand Dependency Injection basics.

2. The Pythonic Twist

In Java, Strategy requires an Interface and implementing Classes. In Python, you just pass functions.

3. Code: Payment System

strategy.py
# Strategies (Just functions)
def pay_via_paypal(amount):
    print(f"Paying ${amount} using PayPal")

def pay_via_stripe(amount):
    print(f"Paying ${amount} using Stripe")

class PaymentProcessor:
    def process(self, amount, strategy):
        # Strategy Injection
        strategy(amount)

# Usage
p = PaymentProcessor()
p.process(100, pay_via_paypal)
p.process(200, pay_via_stripe)

lessons.title: 4.8 Mini Project: Library Management (LLD)
lessons.content:
The Goal

Design a Low-Level System for a Library with `Book`, `Member`, and `Library` classes.

Complete Solution

library_system.py
class Book:
    def __init__(self, isbn, title, author):
        self.isbn = isbn
        self.title = title
        self.author = author
        self.is_available = True

    def __str__(self):
        status = "Available" if self.is_available else "Checked Out"
        return f"[{self.isbn}] {self.title} - {status}"

class Member:
    def __init__(self, member_id, name):
        self.member_id = member_id
        self.name = name
        self.borrowed_books = []

class Library:
    def __init__(self):
        self.books = {}   # isbn -> Book
        self.members = {} # id -> Member

    def add_book(self, book):
        self.books[book.isbn] = book

    def register_member(self, member):
        self.members[member.member_id] = member

    def borrow_book(self, isbn, member_id):
        book = self.books.get(isbn)
        member = self.members.get(member_id)

        if not book or not member:
            print("Invalid Book or Member ID")
            return

        if not book.is_available:
            print("Book not available")
            return

        book.is_available = False
        member.borrowed_books.append(book)
        print(f"Success: {member.name} borrowed {book.title}")

    def return_book(self, isbn, member_id):
        # Implementation left as exercise
        pass

# Simulation
lib = Library()
b1 = Book("101", "Python Crash Course", "Eric Matthes")
lib.add_book(b1)

m1 = Member("M01", "Alice")
lib.register_member(m1)

lib.borrow_book("101", "M01")
lib.borrow_book("101", "M01") # Should fail

lessons.title: 5.1 Functional Programming: Map, Filter, Lambda
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Write anonymous functions with `lambda`.
  • Transform data streams with `map` and `filter`.
  • Understand why List Comprehensions are often preferred.

2. Intuitive Theory

Functional Programming: Treating computation as the evaluation of mathematical functions, avoiding state changes.
Lambda: A single-line, nameless function.

3. Syntax & Code Patterns

functional.py
nums = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]

# Lambda Format: lambda inputs: output
square = lambda x: x * x

# 1. Map (Apply function to all)
# [1, 4, 9, 16, 25]
squares = list(map(lambda x: x*x, nums))

# 2. Filter (Keep if True)
# [2, 4]
evens = list(filter(lambda x: x%2 == 0, nums))

# 3. Reduce (Aggregate)
from functools import reduce
# 1*2*3*4*5 = 120
product = reduce(lambda acc, x: acc * x, nums)

lessons.title: 5.2 Decorators: The Magic Wrapper
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Understand Functions as First-Class Citizens.
  • Create Decorators to modify behavior (`@decorator`).
  • Use `functools.wraps` to preserve metadata.

2. Intuitive Theory

A decorator is a function that takes a function and returns a new function with added capabilities (logging, timing, auth check).

3. Syntax & Code Patterns

decorators.py
import time
from functools import wraps

def timer(func):
    @wraps(func) # Preserves func name/docstring
    def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
        start = time.time()
        result = func(*args, **kwargs)
        end = time.time()
        print(f"{func.__name__} took {end-start:.4f}s")
        return result
    return wrapper

@timer
def heavy_compute():
    # Simulates work
    time.sleep(0.5) 
    print("Done")

heavy_compute()
# Output:
# Done
# heavy_compute took 0.5001s

lessons.title: 5.3 Generators: Memory Efficient Streams
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Use `yield` to create Generators.
  • Understand Lazy Evaluation (Compute on demand).
  • Process infinite streams without crashing RAM.

2. List vs Generator

FeatureListGenerator
Keywordreturnyield
MemoryEntire list in RAMOne item at a time
SpeedFast iterationSlight overhead

3. Syntax & Code Patterns

generators.py
# List Approach (RAM Intensive)
def get_squares_list(n):
    res = []
    for i in range(n):
        res.append(i*i)
    return res

# Generator Approach (RAM Efficient)
def get_squares_gen(n):
    for i in range(n):
        yield i*i # Pauses execution here

# Usage
gen = get_squares_gen(1000000)
print(next(gen)) # 0
print(next(gen)) # 1
# loop consumes rest...

lessons.title: 5.4 Iterators & Iterables: Under the Hood
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Make custom classes iterable (`__iter__`, `__next__`).
  • Understand the difference between Iterable and Iterator.

2. The Protocol

Iterable: Has `__iter__`. Returns an Iterator.
Iterator: Has `__next__`. Returns data or raises `StopIteration`.

3. Custom Range Class

iterator.py
class MyRange:
    def __init__(self, start, end):
        self.current = start
        self.end = end

    def __iter__(self):
        return self

    def __next__(self):
        if self.current >= self.end:
            raise StopIteration
        val = self.current
        self.current += 1
        return val

# Works in for-loop
for i in MyRange(0, 3):
    print(i) # 0, 1, 2

lessons.title: 5.5 Context Managers: Custom 'with' Blocks
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Create classes with `__enter__` and `__exit__`.
  • Use `contextlib.contextmanager` decorator for simple resources.

2. Use Cases

Database connections, File locks, Timer blocks, Temporary directories.

3. Decorator Approach (Easy)

ctx_mgr.py
from contextlib import contextmanager

@contextmanager
def file_manager(filename, mode):
    f = open(filename, mode)
    try:
        yield f
    finally:
        f.close()
        print("File closed automatically")

with file_manager("test.txt", "w") as f:
    f.write("Hello")

lessons.title: 5.6 Packing & Unpacking: *Args & **Kwargs
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Write flexible functions accepting any number of arguments.
  • Unpack lists/dicts into function calls.

2. Syntax & Code Patterns

args_kwargs.py
# 1. Flexible Function
def log(*args, **kwargs):
    print(f"Positional: {args}")
    print(f"Keywords: {kwargs}")

log("Error", 500, user="admin", id=1)
# Positional: ('Error', 500)
# Keywords: {'user': 'admin', 'id': 1}

# 2. Unpacking into call
nums = [1, 2, 3]
print(*nums) # print(1, 2, 3)

data = {"sep": "-", "end": "!"}
print(1, 2, **data) 
# print(1, 2, sep="-", end="!") -> 1-2!

lessons.title: 5.7 Type Hinting: Modern Python
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Use `typing` module (`List`, `Dict`, `Optional`).
  • Static Analysis concepts (mypy).
  • Improve code readability and IDE autocompletion.

2. Before vs After

typing_demo.py
# Ambiguous
def process(items):
    return items[0]

# Typed (Python 3.9+)
def process_modern(items: list[str]) -> str:
    return items[0]

# Complex Types
from typing import Optional, Union

def find_user(id: int) -> Optional[dict]:
    if id == 1:
        return {"name": "Alice"}
    return None

lessons.title: 5.8 Mini Project: Custom Analytics Pipeline
lessons.content:
The Goal

Build a log processing pipeline using Generators (to handle huge files) and Decorators (to time steps).

Complete Solution

pipeline.py
import time
import random

# Decorator for timing
def step_timer(func):
    def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
        t1 = time.time()
        res = func(*args, **kwargs)
        print(f"Step '{func.__name__}' done in {time.time()-t1:.4f}s")
        return res
    return wrapper

# Generator 1: Read lines (Mock)
def log_reader(n):
    print("Reading logs...")
    for i in range(n):
        # Format: "IP,Status,Latency"
        yield f"192.168.1.{i%255},200,{random.randint(10, 500)}"

# Generator 2: Parse
def parse_logs(lines):
    for line in lines:
        parts = line.split(",")
        yield {
            "ip": parts[0],
            "status": int(parts[1]),
            "latency": int(parts[2])
        }

# Logic: Filter high latency
@step_timer
def run_pipeline(n):
    raw_stream = log_reader(n)
    parsed_stream = parse_logs(raw_stream)
    
    # Filter ONLY high latency (functional style)
    slow_requests = (r for r in parsed_stream if r['latency'] > 200)
    
    count = 0
    # Consumes the stream here
    for _ in slow_requests:
        count += 1
    return count

print(f"Slow requests found: {run_pipeline(1000000)}")
# Processes 1M simulated logs without RAM spike!

lessons.title: 6.1 Time & Space Complexity Analysis (Deep Dive)
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Calculate Big O for Recursive and Iterative solutions.
  • Understand "Amortized Analysis" (Why appending to a list is O(1)).
  • Identify O(1), O(log N), O(N), O(N log N), O(2^N).

2. Visual Guide to Growth

O(1): Hash Map Lookup.
O(log N): Binary Search.
O(N): Linear Scan.
O(N log N): Sorting (Timsort/Merge Sort).
O(2^N): Recursion without Memoization (Fibonacci).

3. Syntax & Code Patterns

complexity.py
# O(N) Space & Time
def factorial(n):
    if n == 0: return 1
    return n * factorial(n-1)

# O(1) Space, O(N) Time
def factorial_iter(n):
    res = 1
    for i in range(1, n+1):
        res *= i
    return res

lessons.title: 6.2 Sliding Window Technique
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Recognize problems solvable with Sliding Window (Subarrays/Substrings).
  • Implement Fixed Size vs Variable Size windows.
  • Reduce O(N^2) brute force to O(N).

2. Intuitive Theory

Instead of recalculating the entire subarray sum every time, just subtract the element leaving the window and add the element entering it.

3. Worked Example: Max Sum Subarray of Size K

sliding_window.py
def max_sum_subarray(nums, k):
    max_sum = 0
    window_sum = sum(nums[:k])
    max_sum = window_sum
    
    # Slide
    for i in range(len(nums) - k):
        # Subtract outgoing, Add incoming
        window_sum = window_sum - nums[i] + nums[i+k]
        max_sum = max(max_sum, window_sum)
        
    return max_sum

print(max_sum_subarray([2, 1, 5, 1, 3, 2], 3)) # 9 (5+1+3)

lessons.title: 6.3 Two Pointers Technique
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Solve "Sorted Array" problems efficiently.
  • Implement "Fast & Slow" pointers (Floyd's Cycle Detection).

2. Syntax & Code Patterns

two_pointers.py
# Problem: Check if palindrome (ignoring non-alphanumeric)
def is_palindrome(s):
    l, r = 0, len(s) - 1
    
    while l < r:
        if not s[l].isalnum():
            l += 1
            continue
        if not s[r].isalnum():
            r -= 1
            continue
        if s[l].lower() != s[r].lower():
            return False
        l += 1
        r -= 1
    return True

lessons.title: 6.4 Python's heapq (Priority Queues)
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Use `heapq` for O(log N) push/pop.
  • Solve "Top K Elements" problems.
  • Implement Min-Heap vs Max-Heap (Python defaults to Min).

2. Top K Pattern

heap_demo.py
import heapq

nums = [3, 2, 1, 5, 6, 4]
k = 2

# Efficient way to find Kth largest
# Maintain a min-heap of size K
min_heap = []

for num in nums:
    heapq.heappush(min_heap, num)
    if len(min_heap) > k:
        heapq.heappop(min_heap)
        
print(min_heap[0]) # The Kth largest element (4)

lessons.title: 6.5 Searching & Sorting (Bisect Module)
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Use `bisect` module for binary search (Don't reinvent the wheel).
  • Master Custom Sorting with `key=lambda x: ...`.

2. The Power of Bisect

binary_search.py
import bisect

sorted_scores = [10, 20, 30, 40, 50]

# Where to insert 25 to keep it sorted?
idx = bisect.bisect_left(sorted_scores, 25)
print(idx) # 2 (between 20 and 30)

bisect.insort(sorted_scores, 25)
print(sorted_scores) # [10, 20, 25, 30, 40, 50]

3. Complex Sorting

sort_key.py
data = ["a-1", "b-10", "c-3"]
# Sort by the number after dash
sorted_data = sorted(data, key=lambda x: int(x.split("-")[1]))
# ['a-1', 'c-3', 'b-10']

lessons.title: 6.6 Recursion & Memoization
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Understand the Base Case and Recursive Step.
  • Use `@lru_cache` to optimize recursion (Dynamic Programming made easy).

2. Optimization Magic

memoization.py
from functools import lru_cache

# Without cache: O(2^N) - Terrible for N=40
# With cache: O(N) - Instant
@lru_cache(maxsize=None)
def fib(n):
    if n < 2: return n
    return fib(n-1) + fib(n-2)

print(fib(50)) # 12586269025

lessons.title: 6.7 Linked Lists & Trees (Python Classes)
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Implement Node classes for Lists and Trees.
  • Perform BFS/DFS traversals.

2. Standard Implementations

ds_nodes.py
class ListNode:
    def __init__(self, val=0, next=None):
        self.val = val
        self.next = next

class TreeNode:
    def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None):
        self.val = val
        self.left = left
        self.right = right

# DFS Traversal (Preorder)
def dfs(node):
    if not node: return
    print(node.val)
    dfs(node.left)
    dfs(node.right)

lessons.title: 6.8 Mock Interview: Valid Parentheses
lessons.content:
The Problem

Given a string containing '(', ')', '{', '}', '[' and ']', determine if the input string is valid.

Complete Solution (Stack)

valid_parens.py
def is_valid(s):
    stack = []
    mapping = {")": "(", "}": "{", "]": "["}
    
    for char in s:
        if char in mapping:
            # Closing bracket. Pop check.
            top_element = stack.pop() if stack else '#'
            if mapping[char] != top_element:
                return False
        else:
            # Opening bracket. Push.
            stack.append(char)
            
    return not stack # True if stack is empty

print(is_valid("()[]{}")) # True
print(is_valid("([)]"))   # False

lessons.title: 7.1 NumPy Basics: Arrays vs Lists
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Understand why NumPy is faster than Lists (Contiguous Memory).
  • Create Arrays (`np.array`, `np.zeros`, `np.arange`).
  • Perform Vectorized Operations.

2. Performance Check

Lists: Store pointers to objects scattered in memory.
NumPy Arrays: Store raw data packed continuously (C-Level speed).

3. Syntax & Code Patterns

numpy_basics.py
import numpy as np
import time

# Create Arrays
arr = np.array([1, 2, 3, 4, 5])
zeros = np.zeros(5) # [0., 0., 0., 0., 0.]

# Vectorization (No loops!)
# Python List: [x * 2 for x in nums]
# NumPy: nums * 2
arr_doubled = arr * 2 
print(arr_doubled) # [ 2  4  6  8 10]

# Speed Test
size = 1_000_000
l1 = list(range(size))
n1 = np.arange(size)

t0 = time.time()
l2 = [x ** 2 for x in l1]
print(f"List time: {time.time()-t0:.4f}s") # ~0.3s

t0 = time.time()
n2 = n1 ** 2
print(f"NumPy time: {time.time()-t0:.4f}s") # ~0.003s (100x Faster)

lessons.title: 7.2 Array Manipulation & Broadcasting
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Reshape arrays (`reshape`, `flatten`).
  • Understand Broadcasting rules (adding 1D to 2D).
  • Slice arrays efficiently.

2. Broadcasting Magic

NumPy "stretches" smaller arrays to match larger ones during math operations, without copying data.

3. Syntax & Code Patterns

broadcasting.py
import numpy as np

# Reshaping
grid = np.arange(1, 10).reshape(3, 3)
print(grid)
# [[1 2 3]
#  [4 5 6]
#  [7 8 9]]

# Broadcasting
# Add [1, 2, 3] to EVERY row
result = grid + np.array([1, 2, 3])
print(result)
# [[ 2  4  6]
#  [ 5  7  9]
#  [ 8 10 12]]

# 4. Slicing
# First 2 rows, Column 1
print(grid[:2, 1]) # [2 5]

lessons.title: 7.3 Pandas Series & DataFrames
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Create DataFrames from Dictionaries and Lists.
  • Inspect data (`head()`, `info()`, `describe()`).
  • Select Columns and Rows (`loc`, `iloc`).

2. Intuitive Theory

Series: A column with labels (Index).
DataFrame: A table made of multiple Series sharing an Index.

3. Syntax & Code Patterns

pandas_intro.py
import pandas as pd

data = {
    "Name": ["Alice", "Bob", "Charlie"],
    "Age": [25, 30, 35],
    "Role": ["Dev", "Manager", "Analyst"]
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Inspection
print(df.head(2)) # Top 2 rows
print(df.info())  # Data types & Memory usage

# Selection
print(df["Name"]) # Column -> Series
print(df.iloc[0]) # First Row -> Series
print(df.loc[df["Age"] > 25]) # Filtering

lessons.title: 7.4 Data Cleaning (Nulls & Duplicates)
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Detect Missing Values (`isna()`).
  • Handle Missing Values (`fillna`, `dropna`).
  • Remove Duplicates (`drop_duplicates`).

2. Real World Strategy

Drop: If rows are few or critical data is missing.
Fill: Use Mean/Median (Numbers) or "Unknown" (Text).

3. Syntax & Code Patterns

cleaning.py
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

df = pd.DataFrame({
    "A": [1, 2, np.nan, 4],
    "B": [5, np.nan, np.nan, 8],
    "C": ["x", "y", "x", "z"]
})

# Detect
print(df.isna().sum()) # Count nulls per column

# 1. Fill Nulls
df["A"] = df["A"].fillna(df["A"].mean()) # Fill with Average

# 2. Drop Rows with ANY nulls
# df_clean = df.dropna()

# 3. Remove Duplicates
df = df.drop_duplicates(subset=["C"])

lessons.title: 7.5 Filtering & Conditional Logic
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Filter rows using Boolean Masking.
  • Combine conditions (`&`, `|`, `~`).
  • Use `.query()` for cleaner syntax.

2. Syntax & Code Patterns

filtering.py
df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Product": ["Apple", "Banana", "Cherry", "Date"],
    "Price": [1.5, 0.5, 2.0, 3.0],
    "Category": ["Fruit", "Fruit", "Fruit", "Dried"]
})

# Boolean Masking
# Get Fruits cheaper than $2
cheap_fruits = df[ (df["Category"] == "Fruit") & (df["Price"] < 2.0) ]

# Query Method (SQL-like)
expensive = df.query("Price > 2.0")

# String Operation
starts_with_a = df[ df["Product"].str.startswith("A") ]

lessons.title: 7.6 Grouping & Aggregation
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Master `groupby()` for categorical analysis.
  • Apply multiple aggregates (`sum`, `mean`, `count`).

2. Split-Apply-Combine

1. Split data into groups based on keys.
2. Apply a function to each group.
3. Combine results into a new DataFrame.

3. Syntax & Code Patterns

groupby.py
df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Dept": ["Sales", "Sales", "HR", "HR", "IT"],
    "Emp": ["Alice", "Bob", "Charlie", "Dave", "Eve"],
    "Salary": [5000, 6000, 4000, 4500, 7000]
})

# Average Salary per Department
avg_sal = df.groupby("Dept")["Salary"].mean()
print(avg_sal)

# Detailed Stats
stats = df.groupby("Dept").agg({
    "Salary": ["mean", "max", "min", "count"]
})
print(stats)

lessons.title: 7.7 Merging & Joining Datasets
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Use `pd.merge()` for SQL-style Joins.
  • Understand Inner, Left, Right, and Outer joins.
  • Use `pd.concat()` to stack tables.

2. Visual Guide

Inner: Only matching keys.
Left: All from Left, matches from Right.
Concat: Glueing tables together (top-to-bottom).

3. Syntax & Code Patterns

merging.py
users = pd.DataFrame({"id": [1, 2], "name": ["Alice", "Bob"]})
orders = pd.DataFrame({"id": [1, 2], "amount": [100, 200], "user_id": [1, 2]})

# SQL: SELECT * FROM users JOIN orders ON users.id = orders.user_id
merged = pd.merge(users, orders, left_on="id", right_on="user_id", how="inner")

print(merged)
#    id_x   name  id_y  amount  user_id
# 0     1  Alice     1     100        1
# 1     2    Bob     2     200        2

lessons.title: 7.8 Mini Project: Sales Data Analysis
lessons.content:
The Goal

Analyze a sales dataset to find Top Products, Monthly Trends, and Clean bad data.

Complete Solution

sales_analysis.py
import pandas as pd
import io

# Mock Data (CSV Format)
csv_data = """Date,Product,Revenue,Region
2026-01-01,Laptop,1000,US
2026-01-02,Mouse,20,EU
2026-01-05,Laptop,1000,US
2026-02-01,Phone,500,EU
2026-02-10,Phone,NaN,EU
"""

# 1. Load Data
df = pd.read_csv(io.StringIO(csv_data))

# 2. Clean Data (Drop rows with missing Revenue)
df.dropna(subset=["Revenue"], inplace=True)

# 3. Convert Types
df["Date"] = pd.to_datetime(df["Date"])
df["Revenue"] = df["Revenue"].astype(float)

# 4. Extract Month
df["Month"] = df["Date"].dt.month_name()

# 5. Analysis: Total Revenue by Region
region_rev = df.groupby("Region")["Revenue"].sum()
print("Rev by Region:\n", region_rev)

# 6. Analysis: Top Product
top_prod = df.groupby("Product")["Revenue"].sum().idxmax()
print(f"Top Product: {top_prod}")
# Output:
# Rev by Region:
# EU      520.0
# US     2000.0
# Top Product: Laptop

lessons.title: 8.1 FastAPI vs Flask vs Django: Why FastAPI?
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Understand the shift to Asynchronous Python (`async`/`await`).
  • Compare Micro-frameworks (Flask/FastAPI) vs Batteries-included (Django).
  • Why companies are migrating to FastAPI in 2026 (Performance + Typing).

2. Performance Comparison

FrameworkSpeedType SafetyBest For
DjangoSlowOptionalMonoliths, CMS
FlaskMediumNoSimple scripts
FastAPIVery HighBuilt-inHigh-load APIs, AI

3. Hello World

main.py
# pip install fastapi uvicorn
from fastapi import FastAPI

app = FastAPI()

@app.get("/")
async def root():
    # Automatically returns JSON
    return {"message": "Hello World"}
    
# Run with: uvicorn main:app --reload

lessons.title: 8.2 Path Parameters & Type Validation
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Capture dynamic values from URL.
  • Automatic data conversion (Str -> Int).
  • Automatic Error Handling (422 Validation Error).

2. Intuitive Theory

If you define `item_id: int`, FastAPI will try to convert the URL part to an integer. If I visit `/items/foo`, it fails strictly and safely.

3. Syntax & Code Patterns

path_params.py
from fastapi import FastAPI

app = FastAPI()

@app.get("/items/{item_id}")
async def read_item(item_id: int):
    # item_id is guaranteed to be an INT here
    return {"item_id": item_id, "square": item_id ** 2}

# Usage:
# GET /items/5   -> 200 OK {"item_id": 5, "square": 25}
# GET /items/foo -> 422 Unprocessable Entity

lessons.title: 8.3 Query Parameters & Validation
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Handle optional parameters `?skip=0&limit=10`.
  • Set default values and optional types (`Optional[str]`).

2. Syntax & Code Patterns

query_params.py
from fastapi import FastAPI
from typing import Optional

app = FastAPI()

# URL: /items/?skip=0&limit=10&q=python
@app.get("/items/")
async def read_items(
    skip: int = 0, 
    limit: int = 10, 
    q: Optional[str] = None
):
    return {
        "skip": skip, 
        "limit": limit, 
        "search_query": q
    }

lessons.title: 8.4 Pydantic Models (Request Body)
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Define Data Schemas using Pydantic `BaseModel`.
  • Accept JSON bodies in POST requests.
  • Automatic JSON validation and documentation (Swagger UI).

2. The "Shape" of Data

Pydantic enforces that the data sent by the frontend MATCHES the class definition. No more manual `if 'name' not in data` checks.

3. Syntax & Code Patterns

request_body.py
from fastapi import FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel

class Item(BaseModel):
    name: str
    price: float
    is_offer: bool = None

app = FastAPI()

@app.post("/items/")
async def create_item(item: Item):
    # 'item' is a python object here
    return {
        "name": item.name, 
        "price_with_tax": item.price * 1.2
    }

lessons.title: 8.5 Response Models & Status Codes
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Restrict what data is sent back (Hide passwords).
  • Return proper HTTP codes (201 Created, 204 No Content).

2. Syntax & Code Patterns

response_model.py
from fastapi import FastAPI, status
from pydantic import BaseModel

class UserIn(BaseModel):
    username: str
    password: str

class UserOut(BaseModel):
    username: str
    # password is NOT here

app = FastAPI()

@app.post("/users/", status_code=status.HTTP_201_CREATED, response_model=UserOut)
async def create_user(user: UserIn):
    # Save to DB...
    return user # Pydantic filters out 'password' automatically

lessons.title: 8.6 Error Handling & Custom Exceptions
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Raise `HTTPException` to stop execution.
  • Return consistent error JSON structure.

2. Syntax & Code Patterns

errors.py
from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException

app = FastAPI()

items = {"foo": "The Foo Item"}

@app.get("/items/{item_id}")
async def read_item(item_id: str):
    if item_id not in items:
        raise HTTPException(
            status_code=404, 
            detail="Item not found"
        )
    return {"item": items[item_id]}

lessons.title: 8.7 Dependency Injection (The Superpower)
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Use `Depends` to share logic (Auth, DB connections).
  • Create reusable components.

2. Intuitive Theory

Instead of importing a global DB variable, you ask FastAPI to "Please give me a database session". This makes testing easier.

3. Syntax & Code Patterns

dependencies.py
from fastapi import Depends, FastAPI

def get_db():
    db = "Database Connection"
    try:
        yield db
    finally:
        print("DB Closed")

app = FastAPI()

@app.get("/users/")
async def read_users(db = Depends(get_db)):
    return {"db_status": db}

lessons.title: 8.8 Mini Project: Todo API (CRUD)
lessons.content:
The Goal

Build a full CRUD (Create, Read, Update, Delete) API for managing Todos using In-Memory storage.

Complete Solution

todo_api.py
from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import List, Optional
from uuid import uuid4

app = FastAPI()

# Model
class Todo(BaseModel):
    id: Optional[str] = None
    title: str
    completed: bool = False

# Database (In-Memory)
todos_db = []

@app.post("/todos/", response_model=Todo, status_code=201)
async def create_todo(todo: Todo):
    todo.id = str(uuid4())
    todos_db.append(todo)
    return todo

@app.get("/todos/", response_model=List[Todo])
async def get_todos():
    return todos_db

@app.get("/todos/{todo_id}", response_model=Todo)
async def get_todo(todo_id: str):
    for todo in todos_db:
        if todo.id == todo_id:
            return todo
    raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="Not found")

@app.delete("/todos/{todo_id}", status_code=204)
async def delete_todo(todo_id: str):
    for index, todo in enumerate(todos_db):
        if todo.id == todo_id:
            todos_db.pop(index)
            return
    raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="Not found")

lessons.title: 9.1 SQL Basics for Python Developers
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Understand Tables, Rows, and Columns.
  • Write Raw SQL: `SELECT`, `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE`.
  • Understand Primary Keys (PK) and Foreign Keys (FK).

2. The Big 4 Commands (CRUD)

ActionSQL CommandExample
CreateINSERT INTOINSERT INTO users (name) VALUES ('Alice')
ReadSELECTSELECT * FROM users WHERE id = 1
UpdateUPDATEUPDATE users SET name = 'Bob' WHERE id = 1
DeleteDELETEDELETE FROM users WHERE id = 1

3. Syntax & Code Patterns

basics.sql
-- Create Table
CREATE TABLE users (
    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    username TEXT NOT NULL,
    email TEXT UNIQUE
);

-- Query
SELECT username FROM users WHERE email LIKE '%@gmail.com';

lessons.title: 9.2 Python's Built-in sqlite3 Module
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Connect to SQLite databases without installing anything.
  • Use Cursors to execute queries.
  • Prevent SQL Injection using Parameterized Queries (`?`).

2. Security Alert: SQL Injection

BAD: f"SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = '{user_input}'"
GOOD: cursor.execute("SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = ?", (user_input,))

3. Syntax & Code Patterns

sqlite_demo.py
import sqlite3

# Connect (or create if not exists)
conn = sqlite3.connect("my_database.db")
cursor = conn.cursor()

# Create Table
cursor.execute("CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS todos (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, task TEXT)")

# Insert (Safe Way)
task_name = "Learn Python"
cursor.execute("INSERT INTO todos (task) VALUES (?)", (task_name,))
conn.commit() # Save changes

# Read
cursor.execute("SELECT * FROM todos")
rows = cursor.fetchall()
print(rows) # [(1, 'Learn Python')]

conn.close()

lessons.title: 9.3 SQLAlchemy: Core vs ORM
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Understand what an ORM (Object Relational Mapper) is.
  • Setup SQLAlchemy Engine.
  • Difference between Core (SQL-like) and ORM (Class-based).

2. Intuitive Theory

Raw SQL: You speak the Database's language.
ORM: You speak Python (Classes/Objects), libraries translate it to SQL.

3. Syntax & Code Patterns

sqlalchemy_setup.py
from sqlalchemy import create_engine, text

# 1. Create Engine (The Connection Hub)
# echo=True prints the generated SQL (Great for debugging)
engine = create_engine("sqlite:///example.db", echo=True)

# 2. Connect and Run Raw SQL (Core style)
with engine.connect() as conn:
    conn.execute(text("CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS test (x int, y int)"))
    conn.execute(text("INSERT INTO test (x, y) VALUES (:x, :y)"), [{"x": 1, "y": 2}])
    conn.commit()

lessons.title: 9.4 Defining Models (Declarative Base)
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Define Database Tables as Python Classes (`DeclarativeBase`).
  • Map columns to types (`Mapped`, `mapped_column` - SQLAlchemy 2.0).

2. Modern SQLAlchemy (2.0+)

We use type hints for column definitions. It's cleaner and IDE-friendly.

3. Syntax & Code Patterns

models.py
from sqlalchemy.orm import DeclarativeBase, Mapped, mapped_column
from sqlalchemy import String

class Base(DeclarativeBase):
    pass

class User(Base):
    __tablename__ = "users"
    
    # Primary Key
    id: Mapped[int] = mapped_column(primary_key=True)
    
    # Columns
    name: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(30))
    email: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String)

    def __repr__(self):
        return f""

lessons.title: 9.5 CRUD Operations with ORM (Session)
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Use `Session` to manage transactions.
  • `add()`, `commit()`, `refresh()`.
  • Querying with `select()`.

2. Syntax & Code Patterns

orm_crud.py
from sqlalchemy.orm import Session
from sqlalchemy import select
from models import User, Base, engine 

# Create Tables
Base.metadata.create_all(engine)

with Session(engine) as session:
    # 1. Create
    new_user = User(name="Alice", email="alice@test.com")
    session.add(new_user)
    session.commit()
    
    # 2. Read
    stmt = select(User).where(User.name == "Alice")
    user = session.scalars(stmt).first()
    print(user) # 
    
    # 3. Update
    user.email = "new@test.com"
    session.commit() # Auto-detects changes
    
    # 4. Delete
    session.delete(user)
    session.commit()

lessons.title: 9.6 Relationships (One-to-Many)
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Link Tables using `ForeignKey` and `relationship()`.
  • Access related data easily (`user.posts`).

2. Syntax & Code Patterns

relationships.py
from sqlalchemy import ForeignKey
from sqlalchemy.orm import relationship

class User(Base):
    __tablename__ = "user_account"
    id: Mapped[int] = mapped_column(primary_key=True)
    name: Mapped[str]
    
    # One User has Many Addresses
    addresses: Mapped[list["Address"]] = relationship(back_populates="user")

class Address(Base):
    __tablename__ = "address"
    id: Mapped[int] = mapped_column(primary_key=True)
    email: Mapped[str]
    user_id: Mapped[int] = mapped_column(ForeignKey("user_account.id"))
    
    # Link back to User
    user: Mapped["User"] = relationship(back_populates="addresses")

# Usage
# u1 = User(name="Bob")
# a1 = Address(email="bob@mail.com", user=u1) 
# session.add_all([u1, a1])

lessons.title: 9.7 Async Database Access (AsyncSQL)
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Why do we need Async DBs in FastAPI? (Don't block the loop).
  • Using `AsyncEngine` and `AsyncSession`.

2. Syntax & Code Patterns

async_db.py
# pip install aiosqlite
from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import create_async_engine, async_sessionmaker

# Note the 'sqlite+aiosqlite://' scheme
engine = create_async_engine("sqlite+aiosqlite:///async_db.db")
AsyncSessionLocal = async_sessionmaker(engine)

async def get_users():
    async with AsyncSessionLocal() as session:
        result = await session.execute(select(User))
        return result.scalars().all()

lessons.title: 9.8 Mini Project: Todo Database CLI
lessons.content:
The Goal

Create a Persistent Todo App using SQLAlchemy 2.0 and SQLite.

Complete Solution

todo_cli.py
import sys
from sqlalchemy import create_engine, select
from sqlalchemy.orm import DeclarativeBase, Mapped, mapped_column, Session

# 1. Config
engine = create_engine("sqlite:///todos.db")
class Base(DeclarativeBase): pass

class Todo(Base):
    __tablename__ = "todos"
    id: Mapped[int] = mapped_column(primary_key=True)
    task: Mapped[str]
    done: Mapped[bool] = mapped_column(default=False)

Base.metadata.create_all(engine)

# 2. Logic
def add(task):
    with Session(engine) as s:
        s.add(Todo(task=task))
        s.commit()
    print("Added!")

def list_todos():
    with Session(engine) as s:
        todos = s.scalars(select(Todo)).all()
        for t in todos:
            status = "[x]" if t.done else "[ ]"
            print(f"{t.id}. {status} {t.task}")

def complete(todo_id):
    with Session(engine) as s:
        t = s.get(Todo, todo_id)
        if t:
            t.done = True
            s.commit()
            print("Completed!")

# 3. Simple CLI
if __name__ == "__main__":
    cmd = sys.argv[1] if len(sys.argv) > 1 else "list"
    if cmd == "add": add(sys.argv[2])
    elif cmd == "done": complete(int(sys.argv[2]))
    else: list_todos()

lessons.title: 10.1 The Importance of Automated Testing
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Understand "Confidence Code": Why tests let you refactor safely.
  • Learn the Testing Pyramid: Unit vs Integration vs E2E.
  • Write your first assertion.

2. The "It Works on My Machine" Syndrome

Manual testing is slow, error-prone, and boring. Automated tests run in milliseconds and never forget to check an edge case.

3. Syntax & Code Patterns

first_test.py
def add(a, b):
    return a + b

# Manual Test (Bad)
print(add(2, 3)) # User has to look and verify '5'

# Automated Test (Good)
# If this passes, silence. If it fails, CRASH.
assert add(2, 3) == 5, "Math is broken!"
assert add(-1, 1) == 0
print("All tests passed!")

lessons.title: 10.2 Unit Testing with Pytest
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Install and run `pytest`.
  • Write test functions (`test_...`).
  • Understand Test Discovery.

2. Why Not 'unittest'?

Python's built-in `unittest` requires Classes. `pytest` uses simple functions and powerful asserts. It is the industry standard in 2026.

3. Syntax & Code Patterns

test_app.py
# app_code.py
def divide(a, b):
    if b == 0: raise ValueError("Cannot divide by zero")
    return a / b

# test_app.py
import pytest
from app_code import divide

def test_divide_normal():
    assert divide(10, 2) == 5

def test_divide_zero_raises_error():
    # Assert that a specific Error is raised
    with pytest.raises(ValueError):
        divide(10, 0)

# Run in terminal: pytest

lessons.title: 10.3 Test Fixtures & Setup
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Use `@pytest.fixture` to prepare data/connections.
  • Share setup code across multiple tests.
  • Understand setup/teardown (yield fixtures).

2. Syntax & Code Patterns

fixtures.py
import pytest

class Database:
    def connect(self): print("Connected DB")
    def close(self): print("Closed DB")

# Fixture: Runs BEFORE test, yields data, runs AFTER test
@pytest.fixture
def db_conn():
    db = Database()
    db.connect()
    yield db # Test runs here
    db.close() # Teardown

def test_database_insert(db_conn):
    # db_conn is automatically passed
    assert db_conn is not None

lessons.title: 10.4 Mocking External Dependencies
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Why Mock? (Don't hit real APIs/Credit Cards in tests).
  • Use `unittest.mock.MagicMock`.
  • Patching functions with `@patch`.

2. Intuitive Theory

A "Mock" is a fake object that tracks how it was called. It lies and says "I worked!" without actually doing anything.

3. Syntax & Code Patterns

mocking_demo.py
from unittest.mock import MagicMock

# 1. Simple Mock
fake_api = MagicMock()
fake_api.get_user.return_value = {"name": "Alice"}

print(fake_api.get_user(1)) # {'name': 'Alice'}
fake_api.get_user.assert_called_with(1) # Verified!

# 2. Patching a real import
# Assume 'payment_gateway.charge' calls a bank
from unittest.mock import patch
import payment_gateway

@patch("payment_gateway.charge")
def test_pay(mock_charge):
    mock_charge.return_value = True
    
    # Code that calls payment_gateway.charge()
    result = payment_gateway.charge("123", 100)
    
    assert result is True
    # Ensure real bank was NOT charged
    mock_charge.assert_called_once()

lessons.title: 10.5 Debugging Like a Pro (PDB & IDE)
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Stop using `print()` for everything.
  • Use `breakpoint()` (Python 3.7+).
  • Inspect variables, step over/into code.

2. The Breakpoint

When code hits `breakpoint()`, execution pauses. You get a terminal (pdb) to type variable names and see their values AT THAT MOMENT.

3. Syntax & Code Patterns

debug_session.py
def complicated_math(x):
    y = x * 2
    z = y + 5
    # Debug here
    breakpoint() 
    return z / 0 # Error

measure = complicated_math(10)

# Commands in PDB:
# n (next line)
# c (continue)

The Goal

import pytest

def test_deposit():

    acct.deposit(50)

def test_withdraw_error():

2. The Implementation (To Pass Tests)

bank.py
class Account:
    def __init__(self, balance=0):
        self.balance = balance

    def deposit(self, amount):
        if amount <= 0: raise ValueError("Must be positive")
        self.balance += amount

    def withdraw(self, amount):
        if amount > self.balance:
            raise ValueError("Insufficient funds")
        self.balance -= amount

lessons.title: 11.1 Sync vs Async: The Event Loop
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Understand Blocking (Sync) vs Non-blocking (Async) I/O.
  • How the Node.js/Python Event Loop works conceptually.
  • The `async` and `await` keywords.

2. The Chef Analogy

Sync Chef: Puts pasta in water, stares at it for 10 mins (Blocking).
Async Chef: Puts pasta in water, cuts veggies while waiting (Non-blocking).

3. Syntax & Code Patterns

basics_async.py
import asyncio
import time

# Sync (Blocking)
def make_coffee_sync():
    print("Start Coffee")
    time.sleep(2) # BLOCKS everything
    print("End Coffee")

# Async (Non-blocking)
async def make_coffee_async():
    print("Start Coffee")
    await asyncio.sleep(2) # Yields control back to loop
    print("End Coffee")

# Running it
async def main():
    await make_coffee_async()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    asyncio.run(main())

lessons.title: 11.2 Running Tasks Concurrently (`asyncio.gather`)
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Run multiple async functions AT THE SAME TIME.
  • Understand Total Time = Max(Task Time) instead of Sum(Task Times).

2. Performance Check

If you have 3 API calls taking 2s each:
Sync: 2+2+2 = 6s.
Async: max(2,2,2) = 2s.

3. Syntax & Code Patterns

concurrent.py
import asyncio
import time

async def fetch_data(id):
    print(f"Fetching {id}...")
    await asyncio.sleep(2)
    return {"id": id, "data": "Success"}

async def main():
    start = time.time()
    
    # Run 3 fetches concurrently
    results = await asyncio.gather(
        fetch_data(1),
        fetch_data(2),
        fetch_data(3)
    )
    
    end = time.time()
    print(f"Results: {results}")
    print(f"Total Time: {end-start:.2f}s") # ~2.01s (Not 6s!)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    asyncio.run(main())

lessons.title: 11.3 Async HTTP Requests (aiohttp)
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Why `requests` library is dead in Async apps (It blocks!).
  • Using `aiohttp` ClientSession.
  • Scraping/Fetching 100 urls in seconds.

2. Syntax & Code Patterns

async_http.py
import aiohttp
import asyncio

async def fetch_url(session, url):
    async with session.get(url) as response:
        return await response.text()

async def main():
    urls = ["https://example.com", "https://python.org"]
    
    async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
        tasks = [fetch_url(session, url) for url in urls]
        pages = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)
        print(f"Downloaded {len(pages)} pages")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    asyncio.run(main())

lessons.title: 11.4 Threading vs Multiprocessing vs AsyncIO
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • AsyncIO: Best for Network I/O (APIs, DBs). Single Thread.
  • Threading: Old school I/O. Limited by GIL (Global Interpreter Lock).
  • Multiprocessing: Best for CPU Bound (Data Science, Image Processing). Bypasses GIL.

2. Decision Matrix

Task TypeRecommendationWhy?
Waiting for Web/DBAsyncIOLow overhead, high concurrency
Heavy Math/MLMultiprocessingUses all CPU Cores
Legacy I/OTrendingSimple, but heavy

lessons.title: 11.5 CPU Bound Tasks with ProcessPoolExecutor
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Offload heavy CPU work from the Async Event Loop.
  • Use `run_in_executor` to combine Async with Multiprocessing.

2. The Problem

If you calculate Primes inside an `async def`, you FREEZE the server. No other requests can be handled.

3. The Solution

cpu_bound.py
import asyncio
from concurrent.futures import ProcessPoolExecutor

# CPU Heavy Function (Blocking)
def get_prime_numbers(n):
    primes = []
    for num in range(2, n):
        if all(num % i != 0 for i in range(2, int(num**0.5) + 1)):
            primes.append(num)
    return len(primes)

async def main():
    loop = asyncio.get_running_loop()
    
    # Run in a separate PROCESS (True Parallelism)
    with ProcessPoolExecutor() as pool:
        # Non-blocking await!
        result = await loop.run_in_executor(pool, get_prime_numbers, 1000000)
        
    print(f"Found {result} primes")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    asyncio.run(main())

lessons.title: 11.6 Async Context Managers & Iterators
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Create objects usable with `async with`.
  • Create iterators usable with `async for`.

2. Syntax & Code Patterns

async_context.py
import asyncio

class AsyncDatabase:
    async def __aenter__(self):
        print("Connecting...")
        await asyncio.sleep(0.1)
        return self

    async def __aexit__(self, exc_type, exc, tb):
        print("Closing...")
        await asyncio.sleep(0.1)

    async def query(self):
        return "Data"

async def main():
    # Setup/Teardown is awaited automatically
    async with AsyncDatabase() as db:
        print(await db.query())

if __name__ == "__main__":
    asyncio.run(main())

lessons.title: 11.7 Common Pitfalls & Best Practices
lessons.content:
1. Learning Outcomes

  • Don't call blocking code (time.sleep, requests) in async functions.
  • Avoid "Fire and Forget" tasks (garbage collected/exceptions lost).
  • Use `asyncio.create_task` properly.

2. The "Blocking" Trap

One blocking call freezes the ENTIRE application for ALL users.

3. Correct Task Creation

traps.py
import asyncio

async def background_job():
    print("Job started")
    await asyncio.sleep(1)
    print("Job done")
    return "OK"

async def main():
    # Schedule execution safely
    task = asyncio.create_task(background_job())
    
    # Do other things...
    print("Doing work...")
    
    # Wait for result
    res = await task
    print(res)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    asyncio.run(main())

lessons.title: 11.8 Mini Project: High-Speed Web Scraper
lessons.content:
The Goal

Fetch titles from 20 websites concurrently using `aiohttp` and parse with `BeautifulSoup`.

Complete Solution

scraper.py
import asyncio
import aiohttp
from bs4 import BeautifulSoup

# List of dummy targets
SITES = [
    "https://example.com",
    "https://python.org",
    "https://google.com",
    # ... imagine 20 URLs here
]

async def get_title(session, url):
    try:
        async with session.get(url, timeout=5) as resp:
            text = await resp.text()
            soup = BeautifulSoup(text, "html.parser")
            title = soup.title.string if soup.title else "No Title"
            return f"{url} -> {title.strip()}"
    except Exception as e:
        return f"{url} -> Error: {str(e)}"

async def main():
    async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
        tasks = [get_title(session, url) for url in SITES]
        results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)
        
        for res in results:
            print(res)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    # pip install aiohttp beautifulsoup4
    asyncio.run(main())
